text: BEAR CREEK, a river in the United States, which rises in the state of Alabama, and after a course of 70 m., in which it separates Alabama and Mississippi, enters the Tennessee.—Also a township in Columbia co., Pennsylvania, U.S. Pop. 1,900.—Also a river in Upper Canada, of which the E, or principal branch, rises in the township of London, and runs W to its junction with the N branch, flowing SW and S, 9 m. above their confluence with the St. Clair. Both branches are navigable, the E branch for 9 m., and the N for 5 m. above the forks or point of junction.
BEAR HAVEN. See BEREHAVEN.
BEAR ISLAND (GREAT), an island in Hudson's bay, in N lat. 54° 34', W long. 81° 20'.—Also an island in Onslow bay, off the coast of North Carolina.
BEAR ISLE (South and North), two islands in Hudson's bay; the former in N lat. 54° 32'; the other in 54° 46'.
BEAR ISLE. See CHERRY ISLE.
BEAR ISLES, a group in the N Polar sea, between N lat. 70° 52' and 70° 37', E long. 160° 24' and 162° 24'. They are inhabited by foxes, wolves, bears, and reindeer.

BEAR LAKE, a small lake in the Peak mountains of N America, in N lat. 56° 30', W long. 124°, which discharges itself into an affluent of Simpson's river flowing W to the Pacific.—Also an expansion of the Missinippi or Churchill river, in N America, in N lat. 52° 32', W long. 107° 28'. It is about 35 m. in length, and is full of islands, rapids, and rocks.

BEAR LAKE (GREAT), a large sheet of water, in the NW part of North America, near the shores of the Arctic ocean. It is of very irregular figure, consisting of five arms or bays, which meet in a common centre, from 100 to 150 m. in diam. Fort Franklin, near its outlet, or SW extremity, is in N lat. 65° 12', W long. 123° 13'. Its surface is about 200 ft. above sea-level, but its depth is much greater,-probably as much below sea-level. As it maintains its level all the winter, while the superficial supplies are cut off by the frost, it is evident that its chief sources must be at the bottom of the lake. Its waters are of a light blue colour, and so transparent that a piece of white rag, when sunk in them, did not disappear till it had reached a depth of 90 ft. Its principal tributary stream is Dease river, which enters its NE arm. It discharges its waters by Bear Lake river, which issues from Keith bay, and flows in a SW direction for 70 m. to the Mackenzie river, which it joins in N lat. 64° 59′, 500 m. above its mouth. Bear Lake river has an average breadth of 450 yards, with a depth of from 3 to 10 ft., and a flow of 6 m. per hour. The lake abounds in fish, amongst which are trout, carp, and a species called the herring-salmon. In 1825 it was completely frozen over on the 6th of December; and on the 20th of June next year, the ice was broken up, and began to drift down the river.- Franklin.—Richardson.

BEAR RIVER, the principal tributary to the Great Salt lake, in the Oregon territory of N America. It flows through a beautiful valley from 3 to 4 m. in breadth, perfectly level, and bounded by mountainous ridges which rise suddenly from the plain, and tower successively above each other as they recede from it. In N lat. 42° 03′ 47″, W long. 111° 10′ 53", Fremont calculated the height of this valley to be 6,400 ft. above sea-level. The general course of the river is to the W of S; and it falls into the Great Salt lake on its NE shore, in N lat. 41° 32'.
BEAR SOUND, or BAER SOUND, an inlet of Davis' straits, on the W coast of Greenland, in N lat. 63° 21'.
BEARD, a township in Glossop p., Derbyshire, 10 m. N by W of Chapel-en-le-Frith. Pop. 290.
BEARDSTOWN, a village of Illinois, U.S., in Cass co., on the E side of Illinois river, 48 m. W by N of Springfield. Pop. 336.
BEARFIELD, a township of Ohio, U. S., in Perry co. Pop. 1,455.
BEARL, a hamlet in the p. of Bywell-St.-Andrew, Northumberland, 2 m. N of Bywell. Pop. 36.
BEARLOO, or BAERLO, a town in Prussian Limburg, 3 m. SW of Venlo.
BEARLEY, a chapelry in Warwickshire, 4 m. NNW of Henley-in-Arden. Area 810 acres. Pop. 231.

BE'ARN, an ancient prov. of France, lying at the roots of the Pyrenees, and forming part of the basin of the Adour. Its cap. was Pau, on the Gave de Pau; and its other principal towns Oleron, Sainte- Marie, Navarrens, Lescar, and Orthez. Its area of 500,705 hectares is now distributed between the departments of Basses-Pyrénées and Landes. With the former 466,875 hectares are incorporated; with the latter, 33,830. The princes of B. in the Middle ages were powerful sovereigns, who maintained their independence against France, Spain, and England; and no government has succeeded in abolishing the ancient fors or laws of B.—Mazure, Hist. du Bearn.
BEARN (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of France, in N lat. 42° 32′ 5″, E long. 3° 5′ 56″. There is a lighthouse upon it, elevated 722 ft. above sealevel.
BEARSTED, a parish in Kent, 2½ m. E of Maidstone. Area 640 acres. Pop. 605.
BEARSTON, a township in the p. of Mucklestone, Staffordshire, 5 m. NE of Market-Drayton. Pop. 101.
BEARWARDCOTE, a township in the p. of Etwall, Derbyshire, 6 m. SW of Derby. Pop. 36.

BEAS, or BEEAH, a river of India, in the Punjab. It rises in N lat. 32° 34', E long. 77° 12', and after a S course to Mundi of about 100 m., has a width of from 150 to 200 yards, with a depth of 12 ft. From Mundi to Nadan, it has a W course of about 50 m., during which it receives the Hulka and the Binoa. From Nadan it flows NW, enters the Punjab in N lat. 32° 5′, turns S, and joins the Sutledge at Endreesa, in N lat. 31° 12', E long. 74° 56'. Macartney regards it as a larger river than the Sutledge, though of inferior length of course; but Burnes is of opinion that the Sutledge has also the greater volume of water. It is supposed to be the Hyphasis of Arrian.
BEASCOOR, a village of Bornu, on the l. bank of the Yeou, in N lat. 13° 1'.

BEAT (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Saint- Gaudens.—The cant. comprises 25 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,135.—The com. is 12 m. S of Saint-Gaudens, on the Garonne. Pop. 1,272. It contains quarries of fine marble, and conducts a considerable trade with Spain, in horses, cattle, and mules.
BEATA (CAPE), a promontory on the S coast of the island of Hayti, in N lat. 17° 42', W long. 71° 18'.—A small island of the same name lies off it.
BEATH, a parish in the Dunfermline district of BEA Fifeshire, 18 m. from Edinburgh. Pop. in 1841, 973, of whom 127 were in the v. of Cowden-Beath.

BEATENBERG (SAINT), a mountain in the Swiss cant. of Berne, in the district of Interlaken, rising from the shore of Lake Thun. Alt. 3,670 ft. —There is a village of the same name at its foot, on the shores of the lake. Pop. 794.
BEATTYSTOWN, a township in New Jersey, U. S., in Warren co., 58 m. N of Trenton.
BEAUBASIN, or BEAUSIN PORT, a good harbour on the coast of Tierra del Fuego, in the strait of Magalhaens, in S lat. 54° 22', W long. 71° 13'.
BEAUBE'RY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Saint-Bonnet-de-Joux. Pop. 1,015.

BEAUCAIRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Nimes.—The cant., comprising 4 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 13,867.—The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Rhone, opposite Tarascon, 432 m. SSE of Paris, 14 m. E of Nimes, and 6 m. N of Arles. Pop. 9,967. A suspension bridge of 4 arches, and 1,450 ft. long, connects it with Tarascon. The streets of B. are crooked and narrow, but many of the houses are well-built. Its castle is now in ruins. It has a good quay on the Rhone; and by the canal of Beaucaire which joins the Canal du Midi, communicates with the Garonne, the Mediterranean, and the port of Cette. It is also connected by railway with Alais and Nismes. Its manufactures consist of red wines, silks, taffetas, olive oil, and pottery; but its importance as a place of trade is chiefly due to its celebrated fair, which is held annually between the 1st and 28th of July, on a piece of ground, extending between the base of the rock on which the castle stands and the Rhone. This fair was established in 1217, by Raymond VI., Count of Toulouse; and was for many ages attended by merchants and manufacturers from all countries of Europe, and even from Persia and Armenia. It is still attended by vast numbers: so late as 1833, it drew together 60,000 persons, and the amount of business done was said to exceed 150,000,000 francs.
BEAUCAMPS-LE-VIEUX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Hornoy, 24 m. W of Amilus. Pop. 1,452.

BEAUCE, or BEAUSSE, an ancient division of France, in Orleanais. It consisted of three parts, Beauce Proper, Dunois, and Vendomois. Its cap. was Chartres; and its principal towns Nogent-le-Roi, Maintenon, and Bonneval. Its superficial area was 345,987 hectares, forming an extensive level district fertile in corn. It is now comprised in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir.

BEAUCE, a county in Upper Canada, in the district of Quebec, watered by the Chaudière, the Loup, and the Famine. It is 68 m. in length, by 21 m. in average breadth. Its centre is in N lat. 46°, W long. 70° 35′. Area 1,987 sq. m. Pop. in 1831, 10,665.
BEAUCHENE, an island in the Atlantic, to the S of the Falkland islands, in S lat. 53° 10′, W long. 58° 30′.
BEAUCHIEF ABBEY, an extra parochial chapelry in Derbyshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. SW of Sheffield. Pop. 74.
BEAUCLERC, an island in the N Pacific, off the W coast of America, in N lat. 56° 17'.
BEAUCOURT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Delle, 10 m. SSE of Belfort. Pop. 1,355. It is celebrated for its extensive manufacture of clock-work.
BEAUDESERT, a parish in Warwickshire, 1 m. E of Henley-in-Arden. Area 840 acres. Pop. 205.
BEAUDRIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Saint-Germain-du- Plain. Pop. 1,354. #
BEAUFAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of | Sarthe, cant. of Ballon, 12 m. NNE of Le Mans.
BEAUFICEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Sourdeval, 6 m. N of Mortain. Pop. 579.

BEAUFORT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of Lons-le-Saunier.—The cant., comprising 19 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 10,622. The com. had a pop. of 1,178.— Also a canton and com. in the dep. of Maine-et- Loire, arrond. of Baugé. The cant. comprises 7 com., with a pop. of 15,591 in 1831. The com. and v. are on the Couesnon, 18 m. E of Angers. Pop. 5,914. The chief manufacture is that of coarse linens.—Also a town of Sardinia, in Upper Savoy, 11 m. E of Conflans, near the Doron. Pop. 3,000.

BEAUFORT, a county in North Carolina, U. S., in the E part of the State, on Pamlico river and sound. The co. has an area of 670 sq. m. Cotton and rice are its principal productions. Pop. in 1840, 12,225. The cap. is Washington.—Also a district in South Carolina, U. S., in the S extremity of the state, watered by the Combahee, the Savannah, the Coosawhatchie, and other streams, whose mouths form a number of islands along its coasts. Area 1,830 sq. m. Pop. 35,794. The cap. is Coosawhatchie.-Also a port-of-entry in Carteret co., North Carolina, 11 m. NW of Cape Lookout. Pop. 1,100. The harbour is safe, and admits vessels drawing 14 ft. water.—Also a port-of-entry in Beaufort district, in South Carolina, 50 m. NE of Savannah, on the W bank of Port Royal river, a narrow creek of the ocean, 16 m. from the sea. The harbour is spacious; but the bar will not admit of vessels drawing more than 11 ft.

BEAUFORT, a district in the colony of the cape of Good Hope, lying between the parallels of 30° 50', and 33° 20' S; and bounded by the Griquas territory on the N; on the E by the district of Graff Reynet, from which it is separated on the SE by the Salt river; on the S by the district of George, from which it is separated by the Black mountains; and on the W by Tulbagh, from which it is separated by the Rhinoceros river, and by Clanwilliam from which it is separated by the Gross Riet. The Nieuwveld Bergen range intersects it from WSW to ENE, near the centre. Area 20,000 sq. m. Pop. in 1836, 5,908, of whom one-half were whites. Its cap. of the same name is situated in S lat. 32° 20', E long. 22° 48', in the N part of the Great Karroo.

BEAUFORT BAY, an indentation of the Arctic coast in the Esquimaux territory, in N lat. 70° W long. 142°.—Also an anchorage on the W coast of Patagonia, on the E side of Cape Phillip, at the extremity of Smyth channel.

BEAUFORT CAPE, a small promontory on the Arctic coast of Russian America, in N lat. 69° 06' 47", on the depth of a great bay formed between Cape Lisburn and Icy Cape. It is composed of sandstone, enclosing bits of petrified wood and rushes, and is traversed by narrow veins of coal.— Beechey.
BEAUFORT (Port), a harbour of S Africa, at the mouth of the Breede river, in St. Sebastian's bay. It is covered by a bar of sand; but allows vessels of 200 tons to enter.
BEAUFORT'S ISLANDS, a group in Arctic America, at the mouth of Lord Mayor's bay, on the W side of the gulf of Boothia, in N lat. 69° 45', W long. 91° 35'.-Ross.
BEAUFORT (Point), a projecting barren rock, about 800 ft. high, forming the N point of Irby and Mangles bay on the Arctic coast, in N lat. 67° 41' 24", W long. 95° 02' 16".—Back.
BEAUFOU, a commune of France, in the dep. BEA of Vendee, cant. of Le Poire-sous-Bourbon. Pop. 988.
BEAUGAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Cancon. Pop. 1,007.
BEAUGE'. See BAUGE'.

BEAUGENEY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, arrond. of Orleans .- The cant. comprises 7 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 12,760.—The com. is on the r. bank of the Loire, which is here crossed by an ancient bridge of 39 arches, 16 m. SW of Orleans. Pop. 4,883. The leading manufactures of the place are leather, serges, brandy, beetroot sugar, and red wines.

BEAUHARNOIS, a county of Lower Canada, in the district of Montreal, bounded by the St. Lawrence on the NW, and having its centre in N lat. 45° 10', W long. 74° 5'. It is 55 m. in length, by 22 m. in breadth; and has a superficial area of 710 sq. m. Pop. in 1836, 14,164. The principal towns are B., Saint-Regis, and Dundee. The chief rivers are the Chateauguay, English river, Outardes, Norton creek, and Black river.—The seigniory of B. in the above co. has an extent of 254,061 arpents. Pop. in 1836, 7,105. It embraces La Grande Isle in the St. Lawrence, which is 21 m. in circumf., besides several smaller islands. The town or v. of B. is on the E side of an expansion of the St. Lawrence, called the St. Louis. This is one of the most fertile and best cultivated districts in Lower Canada. The B. canal, begun in 1844, commences at this v., and is to extend to a point opposite Coteau du Lac, a distance of 16 m., so as to surmount the obstacles to navigation caused by the several rapids known as the Cascades, the Cedars, and the Coteau du Lac, in which series of falls the St. Lawrence descends about 60 ft.

BEAUJEU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Rhône, arrond. of Villefranche.—The cant. comprises 18 com. with a pop. in 1831 of 18,641.—The town is 12 m. NNW of Villefranche, on the Ardiere. Pop. 2,824. It is the Bellojocum, or Bellojovium of the Middle ages.
BEAUJEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Fresne-Saint-Marnes. Pop. 1,398. There are iron furnaces and forges here.

BEAUJOLAIS, an ancient division of France, in the Lyonnais, now included in the departments of the Loire and the Rhone. The district is traversed by a range of hills extending from the Cevennes northward to the Côte d'Or, and separating the basin of the Loire and the Rhone. It is fertile, and contains some fine timber. The cap. is Villefranche near the Saone. The other principal towns are Beaujeu, Belleville, and Chalieu. Of an area of 179,314 hectares, 117,814 are now comprised in the dep. of the Rhone, and 61,500 in that of the Loire.

BEAULIEU, a river of Hampshire, rising near Lyndhurst, and flowing into the English channel 7 m. E of Lymington.—Also a chapelry and v. on the preceding river, 7 m. NE of Lymington. Area 9,480 acres. Pop. 1,339. On the opposite side of the river are the remains of the monastery of B. founded in 1204, which afforded an asylum to Margaret of Anjou, after the battle of Barnet; and a temporary asylum to the pretender Perkin Warbeck.—Also a parish in co. Louth, 2 m. E by N of Drogheda. Area 1,228 acres. Pop. 688.

BEAULIEU, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Calvados, com. of Caen. Pop. 500. There is a central house-of-detention, or penitentiary, here.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Corrèze, arrond. of Brives. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,916. The com. is on the Dordogne river, 20 m. SE of Brives. Pop. 2,415.—Also a com. in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Aignay-le-Duc. Pop. 242.- Also a com. in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, adjoining Loches, on the r. bank of the Indre. Pop. 2,232.— Also a com. in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. of Vorey. Pop. 1,080.—Also a com. in the dep. of Maineet-Loire, cant. of Thouarce. Pop. 1,008. White wines are made here.-Also a com. in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Triaucourt. Pop. 516. There are bottle-works here.
BEAULIEU-SOUS-BOURBON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of La Mothe-Achard, 8 m. W of Bourbon-Vendée. Pop. 1,248.
BEAULIEU-SUR-LOIRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Chatillon-sur-Loire, 14 m. SE of Gien. Pop. 2,107.
BEAULIEU-SOUS-PARTHENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Mazieres. Pop. 900.

BEAULON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Chevagnes. Pop. 1,472. BEAULY—a corruption of BEAULIEU—a village in the p. of Kilmorack, Inverness-shire, on the N side of the river Beauly, at its confluence with Loch Beauly; 166 m. N by W of Edinburgh, and 121 m. W of Inverness. In the immediate neighbourhood, near the brink of the river, are the remains of the old priory of Beauly, founded, in 1230, for monks of the order of Valliscaulium. The Frasers, Chisholms, Mackenzies of Gairloch, and several other families, have their burial-place here.—About 2 m. W of Beauly are the celebrated falls of Kilmorack.

BEAULY, a river of Inverness-shire, principally formed by the union of the Farrer from Glen- Farrer, and the Glass river which gives name to the entire strath through which the Beauly flows. These two streams unite at Erchless castle; and the conjoined streams then flow NE in a winding course of about 10 m. in length, and with frequent narrowings and widenings to Loch Beauly. The road from Inverness to Beauly is carried across this river by a bridge of 5 arches, with a waterway of 240 ft. There is an excellent salmon-fishery at the mouth of the B., which is navigable by ships of 50 tons burden as far as the village of B.

BEAULY (Loch), the upper basin, or inner division of the Moray frith, into which the Caledonian canal flows. Its northern shores are in Ross-shire; the southern, in Inverness-shire. From Kessock ferry to the mouth of the Beauly river, it is 7 m. in length; its greatest breadth is about 2 m. The shores are low, and well-cultivated.
BEAUMARCHE'S, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Plaisance, 24 m. WSW of Auch, on the Larros river. Pop. 1,679. Red wines are made here.

BEAUMARIS, a parish, borough, and markettown, in the island and county of Anglesea, 239 m. NW of London, and 43 m. from the Menai bridge.— The town is situated on the picturesque bay of B., at the N entrance of the Menai strait. It consists principally of one street, which contains several handsome houses. The principal buildings are the church, the town-hall, the county-hall, the custom-house, and the free-school. Edward I., in 1295, erected a castle here, which is situated at the upper end of the principal street, and the remains of which are very picturesque. B. returned a member to parliament since the reign of Edward VI. It now elects a member in conjunction with the boroughs of Amlwch, Holyhead, and Llangefni. It is likewise the nominationplace in the election of a member for Anglesea. It is not now a place of any importance in a commercial point of view. A few sloops belong to the port, but are chiefly employed in the coasting-trade for other ports. Vessels frequently take shelter in the 20 m. SSW of Fontainebleau. Pop. 1,521. BEA roadstead during tempestuous weather. The bay is a small but safe and sheltered asylum close to the town, which may be entered safely by the W passage at any time of tide. B. has of late been much resorted to, in the summer-season, as a watering-place; and a steam-boat plies during nine months of the year between it and Liverpool. The view from the town commands a fine prospect of a portion of the strait enclosed by the promontory of Orme's head, while the distance is bounded by the Caernarvon mountains which rise ridge on ridge till they terminate in the lofty Snowdon. Pop. of town and p. in 1821, 2,505; in 1831, 2,497; in 1841, 2,701.
BEAUME. See BAUME.

BEAUMES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, arrond. of Orange.—The cant., comprising 7 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 5,028.— The com., on the Salette, 10 m. E of Orange, has a pop. of 1,683. Wine and oil are the chief productions of the district.
BEAUMESNIL, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Bernay .- The cant., comprising 21 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 9,374.— The com., 8 m. SE of Bernay, has a pop. of 451.
BEAUMETZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saume, cant. of Bernaville. Pop. 530.
BEAUMETZ-LES-CAMBRAI, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Bertincourt. Pop. 1,524.

BEAUMETZ-LES-LOGES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Arras.—The cant., comprising 29 com., had a pop. of 12,691 in 1831.—The com. is 6 m. SW of Arras. Pop. 411.
BEAUMONT, a parish in Cumberland, 5 m. WNW of Carlisle, on the Eden river. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 288.—Also a v. in the p. of St. Peter, in the isle of Jersey. Pop. 285.

BEAUMONT, a commune and village of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, arrond. and 20 m. SW of Charleroi. Pop. 1,912. Fine marble is quarried here.—Also a town in Dutch Guayana, SE of Paramaribo.—Also a seigniory of Lower Canada, in the co. of Bellechasse, on the St. Lawrence. Pop. in 1836, 1,036.

BEAUMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardèche, cant. of Valgorge. Pop. 1,340.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Bergerac. The cant. comprises 13 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 8,422. The com., 16 m. ESE of Bergerac, on the Couse, has a pop. of 1,850.—Also a com. in the dep. of Drôme, cant. and 4 m. SSE of Valence. -Also a com. in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Auterive. Pop. 1,339.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Manche, arrond. of Cherbourg. The cant., comprising 20 com., had a pop. of 11,950 in 1831. The com. is 8 m. WNW of Cherbourg. Pop. 884.— Also a com. in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Domevre. Pop. 155.—Also a com. in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. and 2 m. S of Clermont-Ferrand. Pop. 1,858. -Also a com. in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Vouneuil-sur-Vienne, 8 m. SE of Chatellerault. Pop. 1,443.
BEAUMONT-EN-ARGONNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Mouzon, 10 m. ESE of Sedan. Pop. 1,337.
BEAUMONT-EN-AUGE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. and 4 m. W of Pontl'Eveque. Pop. 892.
BEAUMONT-LES-AUTELS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Authon, 8 m. SE of Nogent-le-Rotrou. Pop. 645.
BEAUMONT-EN-GATENAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, arrond. and

BEAUMONT-DE-LOMAGNE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, arrond. of Castelsarrasin. The cant., comprising 18 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 12,770.—The com., 8 m. SSW of Castelsarrasin, on the Gimone, has a pop. of 4,130.
BEAUMONT-CUM-MOZE, a parish in Essex, 7 m. SE of Manningtree. Area 2,890 acres. Pop. 451.

BEAUMONT-SUR-OISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of l'Isle-Adam, on the l. bank of the Oise. Pop. 1,892. It is celebrated for a particular species of cheese called 'fromages de Brie.'

BEAUMONT-DE-PERTUIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Pertuis, 20 m. SE of Apt. Pop. 1,047. BEAUMONT-PIED-DE-BŒUF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Chateau-du- Loir, near the Ire river. Pop. 1,119.

BEAUMONT-LE-ROGER, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Bernay.—The cant., comprising 25 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 15,028.—The com. and v. are on the Rille, 8 m. E of Bernay. Pop. 2,515. There are manufactories here of woollen cloths, leather, and bottles.
BEAUMONT-LA-RONCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, 10 m. N of Tours. Pop. 1,681.
BEAUMONT-VERRON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. and 2 m. NW of Chenoir. Pop. 1,683.

BEAUMONT-LE-VICOMTE, or BEAUMONTSUR-SARTHE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. of Mamers.— The cant., comprising 15 com., had a pop. of 16,655 in 1831.—The com. is 15 m. SW of Mamers. Pop. 2,381.

BEAUNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Montmerault. Pop. 1,171.—Also an arrond., cant., com., and town of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or. The arrond. has an area of 196,959 hectares, and comprises 9 cant., viz., Arnay-le-Duc, B., Bligny-sur-Ouche, Saint-Jean-de-Losne, Liernais, Nolay, Nuits, Pouilly-en-Montagne, and Seurre. Pop. in 1836, 123,030; in 1846, 125,315. The cant., comprising 29 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 26,206. The com. and town are 20 m. SSW of Dijon, on the Bouzevise, a branch of the Meusin, 10 m. from the Saone; in N lat. 47° 1′ 29", E long. 4° 50', at an alt. of 228 metres, or 748 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1789, 8,840; in 1836, 10,678; in 1846, 11,362. It is an ancient town, and possesses an hospital founded in 1443, a public library of 10,000 vols., and a communal college. It is a chief seat of the wine trade of Burgundy, manufactures a large quantity of red wines of the second class, and exports first and second class wines to a large extent; the other manufactures are serges, druggets, leather, cutlery, vinegar, oil, and beet-root sugar. It is the principal grainmarket for the district of Auxois. B. is supposed by some antiquaries, upon very narrow grounds, to be the Bibracte of Cæsar. It appears to have borne in early times the name of Minervia. Eudes III., duke of Burgundy, raised it to the rank of a town in 1203. In 1401 it was nearly entirely consumed by fire. The revocation of the edict of Nantes materially injured its prosperity by drawing from it a great number of Calvinist families whose industry found employment to upwards of 2,000 workmen.

BEAUNE-LA-ROLANDE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, arrond. of Pithiviers.—The cant. comprises 19 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 14,465.—The com., 10 m. NE of Pithiviers, has a pop. of 2,119. Saffron is extensively cultivated in its environs; and a large quantity of honey and wax are annually produced here. BEA

BEAUPORT, a seigniory of Lower Canada, lying along the St. Lawrence, and watered by the Montmorenci and the Beauport. It is 12 m. in length, by 9 m. in breadth. Pop. in 1836, 1,888.—The v. of B. is situated on a gently rising ground within view of the celebrated falls of the Montmorenci. There are extensive saw-mills here. Lake B., or Waterloo, lies about 6 m. from the St. Lawrence.

BEAUPRE'AU, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et- Loire.—The arrond. has an area of 161,221 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 108,518; in 1846, 117,078. It comprises the 7 cant. of B., Champtoceaux, Chemillé, Cholet, Saint-Florent-le-Vieil, Montfaucon, and Montrevault.—The cant. comprises 11 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 17,766.—The com. and town, 30 m. SW of Angers, on the Evre, in N lat. 47° 12′ 7", had a pop. in 1789 of 1,550; in 1836, of 3,288; in 1846, of 3,432. There are tanneries and dye-houses here.
BEAUPRE' ISLES, a cluster of islands in the S Pacific, in the New Caledonian group, in S lat. 21° 28', E long. 167° 30'.
BEAUQUESNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saume, cant. of Doullens. Pop. 2,705.
BEAURAING, a commune and town of Belgium, in Namur, in the arrond. and 14 m. SE of Dinant. Pop. 800.
BEAURAINVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Campagne-les- Hesdin. Pop. 1,163.
BEAURECH, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Creon. Pop. 634.

BEAUREGARD, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Trevoux, 2 m. E of Villefranche, on the l. bank of the Saone. Pop. 346.— Also a hamlet, in the dep. of Allier, com. of Le Veurdre.—Also a com. in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Terrasson. Pop. 1,313.—Also a com. in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Bourg-du-Peage. Pop. 1,651.
BEAUREGARD L'EVEQUE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Vertaizon, 10 m. ENE of Clermont, near the Allier river. Pop. 1,427.

BEAUREPAIRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, arrond. of Vienne. The cant., comprising 14 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 11,052. The com. is 14 m. SE of Vienne. Pop. 2,138.—Also a canton and commune in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire. The cant., comprising 7 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 9,466. The com. is 8 m. ENE of Louhans. Pop. 790.—Also a com. in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Les Herbiers. Pop. 1,056.
BEAUREVOIR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Le Catelet, 10 m N of Saint- Quentin. Pop. 1,390.
BEAURIEUX, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Craonne, near the Aisne, 14 m. SSE of Laon. Pop. 884.

BEAURIVAGE, a river of Lower Canada, which falls into the Chaudiere 4 m. above its confluence with the St. Lawrence. It is nowhere navigable; but in spring and autumn, when swollen by the rains, has a very powerful current.
BEAUSALL, a hamlet in the p. of Hatton, in Warwickshire, 4 m. NW of Warwick. Pop. 292.
BEAUSSAULT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Forges. Pop. 1,131.

BEAUSSET (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Toulon.- The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,899.— The com. and town are 8 m. NW of Toulon. Pop. 3,326. Olive oil, red wines, brandy, and soap, are manufactured here.
BEAUTIRAN, or BAUTIRAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Labrede, 10 m. SSE of Bordeaux. Pop. 863. White and red | wines are manufactured here.

BEAUVAIS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and city of France, in the dep. of the Oise .- The arrond, has an area of 193,491 hectares, and comprises 11 cant., viz., Auneuil, B., Chaumont-en- Vexin, Coudray-Saint-Germer, Formerie, Grandvilliers, Marseille, Meru, Nivillers, Noailles, and Songeons. Pop. in 1836, 132,369; in 1846, 133,837.— The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 19,704 in 1831.—The com. and town are on the road from Paris to Abbeville, 42 m. N of Paris, on the Therain, an affluent of the Oise, in N lat. 49° 26', E long. 2° 4'. Pop. in 1789, 13,082; in 1836, 13,082; in 1846, 12,221. It is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Reims, whose dio. comprises the dep. of L'Oise. Its unfinished cathedral—of which the choir is particularly admired-is its principal building. The town itself is ill-built; and some of the houses are constructed merely of wood. The hotel of the prefecture, formerly the episcopal palace, is a building of some interest. A theological seminary, a communal college, and a public library of 8,000 vols., are among the principal public institutions. B. is chiefly of importance as a manufacturing town. Its royal tapestry manufactory was established in 1664, and is still celebrated. Its linens of the kind called "demi- Hollande" are in repute; and it has extensive manufactories of woollens, besides cotton-printing, dyeing, and bleaching works. Pottery, glass, leather, and toys, are among the minor articles of production. The communal revenue in 1833 amounted to 155,415 francs.-B. is a very ancient town. It was the Civitas Bellovacorum of the Romans. Its bishops were temporal peers, and counts of Beauvais. In 1443 it was unsuccessfully besieged by the English; and in 1472 it was successfully defended against the army of Charles the Bold, when the heroine Jeanne Fouquet, or Jeanne Hachette, at the head of the female citizens of B., greatly distinguished herself by her intrepidity. B. was the birth-place of Marshal Villiers, of Villiers de l'Isle Adam, who defended Rhodes against Soliman I., of the grammarian Restaut, and Vincent de Beauvais.
BEAUVAIS-SUR-MATHA, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Matha. Pop. 1,042.
BEAUVAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. and 4 m. S of Doullens. Pop. 2,302.
BEAUVILLE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, arrond. of Agen .- The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 7,264.—The com. is 15 m. ENE of Agen. Pop. 1,703.
BEAUVOIR-EN-LIONS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Argueil. Pop. 1,443.
BEAUVOIR-DE-MARC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Saint Jean-de-Bournay. Pop. 1,273.

BEAUVOIR-SUR-MER, a canton, commune, and town of France, in Vendée, arrond. of Les Sables.— The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,901.— The com. and town are 30 m. NNW of Les Sables, within 2 m. of the coast, opposite the isle of Noirmoutiers. It has a small port, from which grain and salt are exported. Pop. 2,356.
BEAUVOIR-SUR-NIORT, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Deux Sevres, arrond. of Niort.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,193.—The com. is 6 m. S of Niort. Pop. 491.
BEAUVOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Vermand. Pop. 694.-Also a commune in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Carnieres. Pop. 913. BEA
BEAUVOISIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Vauvert. Pop. 1,269.
BEAUZE'E, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Triaucourt, on the Aire, 15 m. N of Bar-le-Duc. Pop. 805.
BEAUZELY (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron.—The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,023.—The com. is 8 m. NW of Millau, on the Muse. Pop. 1,911.
BEAUZIRE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Ennezac. Pop. 1,118.

BEAVER, a county of Pennsylvania, U.S., in the W part of the state, on the Ohio and the Beaver rivers. Area 548 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 29,368. Its agricultural productions are Indian corn, buck-wheat, rye, potatoes, and sugar. A bituminous coal is wrought in it.—The cap., of the same name, is situated on the N bank of the Ohio, at the confluence of the Big Beaver river, 30 m. below Pittsburg. Pop. 551. -Also a township in Crawford co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 734.—Also a township in Venango co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,611.—Also a township in Pike co., Ohio, 73 m. S of Columbus. Pop. 1,099.—Also a township in Union co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 2,609. -Also a township in Columbiana co., Ohio. Pop. 1,973.—Also a township in Guernsey co., Ohio. Pop. 1,842.—Also a township in Boone co., Illinois. Pop. 236.

BEAVER (Big), a river of Pennsylvania, U. S., formed by two main branches,—the Mahoning, which rises in Ohio,—and the Shenango, which rises in Pennsylvania. The united stream has a course of 20 m. to the Ohio, into which it flows near the town of Beaver; but the length of the river from its sources is about 80 m. The Pennsylvania and Ohio canal, which connects the Ohio at Beaver, with the Ohio canal at Akron, passes along the valley of this river.

BEAVER CREEK, a township in Green co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., on the Little Miami. Pop. 1,767.—Also a township in Washington co., Maryland.—Also a township in Henry co., Ohio.—Also a township in Surrey co., North Carolina.-Also a township in Anderson co., Kentucky.-Also a township in Bond co., Illinois.

BEAVER CREEK, a river in the state of New York, U. S., which falls into the Papachton after a SW course of 35 m.—Also a river in Upper Canada, in the township of Caistor, flowing into the Welland. —Also a river in the township of Humberstone, Upper Canada, falling into Lake Erie.—Also a river in Clarke co., in Ohio, U. S., flowing into Buck creek. -Also a river in Green co., in Ohio, an affluent of the Miami.

BEAVER DAM, a township in Erie co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,081.—Also a township in Ann Arundel co., Maryland, 18 m. from Annapolis.—Also a township in Goochland co., Virginia, 24 m. W o. Richmond.—Also a township in Anson co., North Carolina, 161 m. SW of Raleigh.—Also a township in Union district, South Carolina, 58 m. NNW of Columbia.—Also a township in Randolph co., Alabama, 172 m. E of Tuscaloosa.
BEAVER DAM FORKS, a village in Tipton co., Tennessee, 176 m. WSW of Nashville.
BEAVER ISLANDS, a group of five or six islands in Lake Michigan, U. S. The largest, Big Beaver, contains 40 sq. m.
BEAVER LAKE, an expansion of the Mississippi river, between Pine Island lake and Lake Heron, in N lat. 54° 30', W long. 102°. It is 12 m. in length, and 6 m. wide, and is filled with islands.
BEAVER (LITTLE), a river in the state of Ohio, U. S., which flows into the Ohio, in a S course through the co. of Columbiana, 43 m. below Pittsburg.
BEAVER MEADOW, a village in Northampton co., Pennsylvania, U. S., 102 m. NE of Harrisburg. Excellent anthracite coal is wrought here.
BEAVER TOWN, or BEAVERTON, a thriving village of Upper Canada, in the township of Thorah, on Lake Simcoe, opposite Thorah island.
BEAVER VALLEY, a township in Columbia co., Pennsylvania, U. S., 92 m. N by E of Harrisburg.
BEAWORTH, a tything in the p. of Fawley, Hampshire, 43 m. S of New Alresford. Pop. 153.
BEAWORTHY, a parish in Devonshire, 6 m. SW of Hatherleigh. Area 6,270 acres. Pop. 405.
BEAZA, or BAEZA, a town of Ecuador, on the r. bank of the Coca, a branch of the Napo, 70 m. ESE of Quito.
BEBAWAN, a rugged pass in the High Atlas, between Marocco and Tarudant.
BEBBINGTON, a parish in Cheshire, 6 m NE of Great Neston, stretching along the river Mersey. Area 5,250 acres. Pop. 5,008.
BEBBINGTON (HIGHER and LOWER), two townships in the above p., the former 5 m., the latter 51 m. E of Great Neston. Pop. of Higher B. 844; of Lower B. 1,187.
BEBBROW, a village of Prussia, in Pomerania, in the circle of Köslin, N of Lauenburg.

BEBEDER, or Bevedero (Lake), a sheet of water in the intendancy of San Luis, in La Plata, 35 m. SW of San Luis de la Punta, intersected by the parallel of 32° 50' S. It forms the N section of a curious interlacement of marshes, lakes, and streams, formed by the waters of the Desaguadero and the Timuyan. BEBEHAN, BEBUHAN, or BIHBIHAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Shiraz, the cap. of the mountainous district of Kojiluwah, 153 m. from Shiraz, by a road which leads over lofty mountains, and through narrow defiles. The interior of B. more resembles a European village than a Persian town; each house standing alone, and many having small gardens attached. The palace of the beglerbeg is a respectable edifice and tolerably well fortified. The pop. of B. does not exceed 6,000 or 7,000, of whom 2,000 are military.—Kinneir.—Stocqueler.
BEBEJI, a town of Haussa, in W Africa, to the E of the Aushin mountains, in E long. 7° 37', N lat. 11° 30′.
BEBENHAUSEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of Tubingue. Pop. 152. The fine remains of a Cistercian abbey, founded in 1183, exist here.
BEBITZ, a village of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Merseburg, N of Könnern. Pop. 115.
BEBLENHEIM, or BEWLE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Kaysersberg. Pop. 1,096.
BEBRA, a town of Lower Hessen, on the E bank of the Fulda, in the bailiwick of Rothenburg.

BEBRE, a river of France, an affluent of the Loire, which it joins on the l. bank, in the dep. of the Allier. It rises in the dep. of the Loire, and has a course of 45 m., passing La Palisse, Jalligny, and Dompierre.
BEBREWO, or Bebrova, a town of Turkey, in Bulgaria, at the foot of the Hæmus, 27 m. E of Tirnova.
BEBSIDE, a township in Horton p., Northumberland, 5 m. SE of Morpeth. Pop. 91.
BEBUTANG, a village on the N coast of the island of Java, to the N of Jeribon.
